coredata.dc:title: Encapsulation of amaranth CDs at ZIF-7 MOFs as a novel adsorbent for ultrasonic-assisted dispersive nano-solid-phase microextraction and ultrasensitive determination of allura red in food samples
coredata.dc:creator: Esmail, Lawen Abdulsatar
Jabbar, Hijran Sanaan
coredata.dc:description: Allura Red AC (AR) can be used in foodstuffs to give them a pleasant taste, color, appearance, stability, and smell. High consumption of the dye has been associated with adverse health effects. A sensitive method, namely ultrasound-assisted coupled dispersive nano solid-phase micro-extraction spectrophotometry (UA-DNSPME-UV–Vis) was designed for AR as food colorant preconcentration and determination in real samples. The method is based on the first-time usage of amaranth carbon dot@Zn (II) organic framework comprising benzimidazole (ZIF-7) composite. The characterization of the synthesized CD@MOF was performed using XRD, SEM, FTIR, HRTEM, EDX, SAED, UV–Vis absorption, and fluorescence. Among conventional parameters including pH, amount of sorbent, sonication time and type and volume of elution solvent was examined. The established method showed excellent linearity over the range of 0.30 – 7.00 nM. Anions and cations were also studied for their interference effect. The preconcentration factor was 85, with 0.60 nM LOD. As-synthesized nanocomposite had significant adsorption capacity and reusability. Analysis of some real samples showed satisfactory results in the range of 8.41 to 2953 mM for liquid food samples and from 2.95 to 5.88 mM for solid food samples with a satisfactory range of recovery values from 98.44 to 102.41 %.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Allura Red AC (E129)
Amaranth CDs
CDs@ZIF-7
Preconcentration
Ultrasound assisted extraction

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preconcentration of the dye (Ultrasonic-assistant dispersive NP solid-phase micro-extraction)
Optimization of CD@MOF based dispersive-SPE parameters

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration showing CDs, and CDs@ZIF-7 synthesis and determination of AR dye in real samples.
(a) UV–visible absorption spectra of the pure ZIF-7, CDs, and CD@MOF; (b) XRD patterns of CD, ZIF-7 and CD@MOF; (c) EDX of CD; (d) EDX of CD@MOF composite.
(a) SEM image of CDs; (b) SEM image of CD@MOF; (c, d) HRTEM images of CDs; and (e, f) HRTEM images of CD@MOF.
(a) SAED of CD; (b) SAED of CD@MOF; and FTIR spectrs of (d) CD@ZIF-7; (e) CD; (f) ZIF-7.

(a) Absorption spectra of AR dye before (blue) and after (red) ultrasound assisted extraction; effect of (b) time; (c) pH; (d) CDs@MOF dosage; (e) elution solvent; and (f) recovery efficiency during five repetition usage of CD@MOF. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Calibration graph for the determination of AR dye using proposed method.
Accuracy and precision of the proposed extraction method (n = 5).
Influence effects of some ions on the recoveries of AR dye on CD@MOF.
Allura red contents of food samples, and addition tests for determination of analyte.
Analytical performance comparison of several adsorbents for solid phase extraction determination and preconcentration of AR.

head.ce:title: Encapsulation of amaranth CDs at ZIF-7 MOFs as a novel adsorbent for ultrasonic-assisted dispersive nano-solid-phase microextraction and ultrasensitive determination of allura red in food samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Salahaddin University-Erbil, Erbil, Kurdistan Region, Iraq
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Salahaddin University-Erbil, Erbil, Kurdistan Region, Iraq

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Allura Red AC (AR) can be used in foodstuffs to give them a pleasant taste, color, appearance, stability, and smell. High consumption of the dye has been associated with adverse health effects. A sensitive method, namely ultrasound-assisted coupled dispersive nano solid-phase micro-extraction spectrophotometry (UA-DNSPME-UV–Vis) was designed for AR as food colorant preconcentration and determination in real samples. The method is based on the first-time usage of amaranth carbon dot@Zn (II) organic framework comprising benzimidazole (ZIF-7) composite. The characterization of the synthesized CD@MOF was performed using XRD, SEM, FTIR, HRTEM, EDX, SAED, UV–Vis absorption, and fluorescence. Among conventional parameters including pH, amount of sorbent, sonication time and type and volume of elution solvent was examined. The established method showed excellent linearity over the range of 0.30 – 7.00 nM. Anions and cations were also studied for their interference effect. The preconcentration factor was 85, with 0.60 nM LOD. As-synthesized nanocomposite had significant adsorption capacity and reusability. Analysis of some real samples showed satisfactory results in the range of 8.41 to 2953 mM for liquid food samples and from 2.95 to 5.88 mM for solid food samples with a satisfactory range of recovery values from 98.44 to 102.41 %.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In food industry processes, food additives play a crucial role in maintaining or improving food nutritional value, taste, freshness, and safety [1]. Approximately 70 % of dyes are azo compounds, which contain at least one azo chromophores (-N = N–) attached to naphthalene and/or benzene rings with lateral groups –SO3H and –OH [2,3]. Allura Red AC (AR) is an azo-based, red synthetic dye often used to dye fabrics, foods, and cosmetics. It has the European number (E129) and a dark red powder appearance [4]. It is widely applied in drinks, cakes, candy, ice cream, candy coating, and other foods [5]. It can be used in foodstuffs to give them smell, taste, color, stability, a nice appearance, and flavor [6]. Many products contain this ingredient, including tattoo inks. Petroleum is now the main ingredient in AR, which was originally manufactured from coal tar [7]. The consumption of Allura Red by children is not recommended in Europe. It has a negative impact on human and animal behavior, especially in children, and can cause hyperactivity [8]. Consuming of high amounts of synthetic food dyes, especially AR through foodstuffs by humans, as it reported, leads to allergy-like reactions, allergy, eczema, asthma, and cancer-causing symptoms in adults [9]. In addition, it leads to skin irritation, redness, hyperactive behavioral disorders and deficiency of attention in children have been observed [10]. Following absorption through the gastrointestinal tract, Allura Red is transported to the bloodstream, where it interacts with proteins as it is transported and metabolized [11]. The World Health Organization (WHO) has established an acceptable daily intake (ADI), which is a limit on the amount of ingredients a person can consume based on their body weight. The ADI value of Allura red is 7 mg/kg [10].

Allura red's potential toxicity and pathogenicity make its determination in real samples, including drinks, candy, ice cream, and water samples, very important. In real samples, allura red concentrations are extremely low, so an enrichment step before analysis is necessary to achieve a final concentration level compatible with the technique's limits of detection. The interference effects of matrix components of a sample are also a problem in the determination of allura red. Solvent extraction, solid phase extraction, etc., have been used as pre-concentration and separation techniques to resolve these problems [7].

In appropriate solvents, metal ions are combined with organic ligands to form metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) that are permanently microporous materials. Studies on MOFs have recently focused on constructing MOFs with permanent porosity for sensors, ion-exchange, separations, and catalysis [12]. Since MOFs host a wide range of functional species, they offer the potential to develop new types of composite materials with enhanced or new functions compared to their parent MOF counterparts [13]. As a result of their numerous advantages, including extremely large surface areas, tunable pore sizes, and adjustable internal surface properties, MOFs are becoming one of the fastest-growing fields in the chemical and material sciences. They also have a lot of potential for applications [14]. A MOF that contains zinc tetrahedrally coordinated to nitrogen atoms of benzimidazole molecules is the zeolitic imidazolate framework material (ZIF-7) [15]. Recent developments in the encapsulation of nanoparticles into zeolitic imidazolate framework materials have enabled the well-dispersed incorporation of several types of nanoparticles [13].

CDs (carbon dots) consist of small carbon cores stabilized by functional surfaces and belong to the category of carbon nanomaterials with a size less than 10 nm. Considering the small pores size of CDs, embedding CDs inside MOFs is feasible without affecting their structure and physicochemical properties [16]. By encapsulating molecules or nanoparticles with appropriate characteristics, MOFs can act as the host for guest molecules or nanoparticles. By combining the properties of the guest with those of the MOF host, their behavior can be enhanced [17].

Dye quantitation in food samples has been done using several different methods, each with its own advantages and disadvantages. In some cases, expensive equipment is required, and some methods are not very sensitive [18]. To detect dyes in various samples with high sensitivity, it would be necessary to invent an economical method that uses a cheap and widely available device. Due to its numerous advantages, the dispersive micro-solid phase extraction method (DM-SPE) has attracted researchers' attention. Two steps are involved in this method: dispersing the solid adsorbent particles and then separating them. In addition to the large surface area to volume ratios, high extraction efficiencies, rapid extraction kinetics, adsorbent amounts reduction, and toxic organic solvents, nano-materials are also highly effective as nano-adsorbents [19]. Using ultrasound-assisted extraction (UA) can reduce extraction time by mixing the sorbent with sample solution in an efficient way, while improving concentration gradients and analyte diffusion coefficients. In particular, ultrasound assisted dispersive nano-solid phase micro-extraction (UA-DNSPME) that is high linear range, high sensitivity, fast, and simple operation suitable for the monitoring of different analytes at various levels, particularly phenolic compounds [20].

In this study, CD@MOF nanocomposite have been used for ultrasound assisted dispersive nanoparticle solid phase microextraction and preconcentration of the Allura Red AC dye in food colorant and water samples followed by its determination using spectrophotometric method. The carbon dots were synthesized for the first time by carbonizing amaranth (E123). Furthermore, optimal adsorption conditions were obtained through experimental design.

Zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O) obtained from (Sigma-Aldrich, Germany), benzimidazole (C7H6N2,) from (Sigma-Aldrich, Germany), and N,N-dimethylformamide ((CH3)2NCHO, DMF) from (Panreac, Spain). Acetone, chloroform, propanol, ethanol, and methanol were obtained from (Merck, Germany). HNO3, H2SO4, HCl, NaOH were analytical grade and obtained from (Fluka, Germany) without any further purification. Allura Red AR stock solution (1 mM) was prepared from standard powder (Merck, Germany). Low concentrations were obtained by repeated dilutions of the stock solution.

The pH measurements were carried out using inoLab Terminal −740P (inoLab, Germany). The UV–vis absorbance spectra were recorded using an EMC-11D-V spectrophotometer (EMCLAB, Germany). Transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) images were taken using a Philips CM-120 (Philips, Netherlands). TESCAN MIRA III, Czech was used to obtain SEM and EDX images. A Perkin-Elmer spectrometer (PerkinElmer, USA) was used to record the FT-IR spectra. XRD patterns were obtained using a Philips x-ray diffractometer PW 1730 (Philips, Netherlands).

A hydrothermal method was used to synthesize CDs using amaranth as a carbon precursor for the first time with some modifications [21]. For a typical synthesis, one gram of amaranth [Trisodium (4E)-3-oxo-4-[(4-sulfonato-1-naphthyl)hydrazono]naphthalene-2,7-disulfonate] was added to 40 mL ultrapure water and stirred for 30 min. The mixture was then transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, where it was heated at 180 °C for 4.0 h. After centrifugation (8000 rpm, 15 min), the suspension was filtered through a 0.2 μm micro-syringe.

With minor modifications, the composite was prepared using a method published in the literature [15,22]. Typically, zinc nitrate hexahydrate (0.9870 mmol) was dissolved in 20 mL of water and benzimidazole (7.904 mmol) was dissolved in 20 mL of N,N-dimethylformamide. Zinc nitrate solution was rapidly poured into benzimidazole solution under magnetic stirring, then 0.5 mL of CD solution was mixed and allowed to react at room temperature for 1 h. The white precipitate were collected and washed several times with DMF and water. Finally, the resulted solid was dried at 80 ℃ overnight. Through the same method, pure ZIF-7 was prepared without adding CD solution. Fig. 1 shows general steps of CDs synthesis from amaranth, synthesis of CDs@ZIF-7 composite and AR determination in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preconcentration of the dye (Ultrasonic-assistant dispersive NP solid-phase micro-extraction)
ce:section.ce:para:
Batch-mode adsorption studies were carried out by adding 4.0 mg adsorbent CD@MOF and 10 mL Allura red solution of known concentration (5.00 nM) to a centrifuge tube. The resulting mixture was immersed in an ultrasonic water bath to facilitate the adsorption of AR dye onto the CD@MOF at 20 ℃ for 10 min. The adsorbed analyte was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 5 min to separate two phases, then the analyte was desorbed for 5 min by 500 μL ethanol using an ultrasonic bath at 20 ℃ and centrifuged for 5 min at 5000 rpm. Finally, the ethanol layer was withdrawn by micro syringe and analyte content was analyzed by UV–Vis Spectrophotometer and absorbance intensity of AR was recorded at wavelength of 510 nm. The criterions for optimization are based on enrichment factor (EF) and extraction recovery (ER%) according to equations (1) and (2) [1,19,20].
(1)
EF = C s e d /
C °
(2)
ER % =
Csed × V s e d
/
C °
× V a q
× 100 = E F ×
Vsed
/
Vaq
were Csed and Co are the concentrations of the analytes in the sediment and initial aqueous samples, respectively. From AR standard solution calibration graph, Csed was calculated. The sediment phase and real sample volumes are Vsed and Vaq, respectively.

Samples of different beverages, candy, commercial food colorants, and jelly containing AR dye were purchased from local markets. 10 mL of beverage samples (energy drink, pomegranate flavored drink, and strawberry flavored drink) were directly subjected to the optimized method without applying any pretreatments.
Carbonated beverage sample was heated for 5.0 min to eliminate any dissolved carbon dioxide (CO₂) and used further without any additional treatments.

0.5 g of powdered jelly sample was directly dissolved in distilled water in a 100 mL volumetric flask. 10 mL portion of this solution was applied to the optimized method after filtering through a PTFE membrane filter (0.45 µm).

Candy tablets (red candy: 0.50 g) were weighed directly and dissolved in 20 mL distilled water and then filtered through a PTFE membrane filter (0.45 µm). Necessary dilutions (total volume: 250 mL) were done. The volumes of 10 mL of the candy solution were analyzed with the procedure in section 2.5.

The UV–vis absorption spectra showed in Fig. 2
a , for pure ZIF-7, CDs, and CD@MOF. Carbon dots had absorption peaks at 266 nm and 278 nm, which is similar to the results obtained by reported methods [23]. The π-π* transition of aromatic C = C and C-C bonds which originated from the aromatic π system could be responsible for the peak at 266 nm [24]. A peak at 278 nm may be ascribed to the n-π* transition of the C = O groups since carbon-based nanoparticles were successfully oxidized. Additionally, CD@MOF exhibited specific absorption peaks near 266 and 278 nm, in agreement with CDs. It has been demonstrated that CDs have been successfully encapsulated in ZIF-7′s cavities. While pure ZIF-7 had no absorption bands [22,25].

Analyses of X-ray diffraction (XRD) were performed on the synthesized samples to determine their crystallinity. Fig. 2
b shows the powder XRD patterns of CDs@MOFs, MOFs, and CDs. Amorphous CDs were observed in the XRD pattern, while crystalline MOFs and CDs@MOF were observed. Carbon dots revealed a broad peak at 2θ = 20.0, indicating the amorphous nature of the prepared C-dots [26,27]. Additionally, the broad peak of C-dots was converted into the sharp peak caused by spectacular dispersion of C-dots in DMF during nanocomposite synthesis and resulting in the crystalline nanocomposite. The pre-dominant structure of nanocomposite contained the prominent peaks of carbon dots as well as sharp peaks of the MOFs indicating successful formation of CDs@MOFs [28].

The elemental composition of CDs and CD@ZIF-7 was determined by EDX analysis, as in Fig. 2
c and 2d . According to the elemental composition, the as-synthesized CDs are mostly made of the atoms C, N, and O, which suggests that carbon dots have formed. A trace amount of sodium and sulfur was also found due to the use of Amaranth, which contains these elements. Also the elemental composition of CD@MOF determined which contain Zn beside of C, N, O,Na, and S which further demonstrated the CDs@ZIF-7 was successfully fabricated [29,30].

Scanning electron microscopy was used to investigate the general morphologies of CDs and CDs@MOF composites. A uniform and smooth surface was observed on all synthesized samples, as shown in Fig. 3
a, and 3b
[31]. To establish the morphologies and particle sizes, several HRTEM images were employed for as-synthesized carbon dots and CD@MOF. The as-synthesized CDs are mainly spherical (Fig. 3
c, 3d, 3e, 3f ). The size of the CD is approximately 3.0 nm. As shown the CDs are distributed in the composite without any aggregation.

The SAED pattern is clearly shown in Fig. 4
a and 4b for CD and CD@MOF composite. The diffused ring pattern observed in the SAED for CDs suggested that the presence of amorphous carbon, there are also the spots pattern arising from the CD can be found in SAED of composite further suggesting that CD-MOF hybrids were effectively obtained [32]. According to the FIIR spectrum (Fig. 4
c, 4d, 4e ), CDs@ZIF-7 composites exhibit several characteristic absorption bands associated with their CDs and ZIF-7 parent counterparts. CDs are well encapsulated in ZIF-7 and retain their original excellent properties as a result of these experiments [13,33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of CD@MOF based dispersive-SPE parameters
ce:section.ce:para:
When designing the carbon dot at metal organic framework based DSPE optimization experiments, it was important to develop an extraction/preconcentration procedure that was easy and fast without impacting the recovery of analytes. As a result, pH, adsorbent amount, sonication time, and desorption conditions were investigated and optimized. The effect of each factor on extraction efficiency was evaluated by running batch experiments with 5.00 nM of Allura red. Fig. 5
a shows a significant enhancement of absorbance after extraction with UA-DSPME-UV–Vis, which confirms the applicability of the present method for analyzing trace Allura red.

The incubation time varied between 3 and 21 min. In the first 15 min, recovery values increased and remained constant above this point as shown in Fig. 5
b . A 15-minute equilibration time was applied to all subsequent experiments based on the results of this experiment. In any solid-phase micro extraction procedure, pH plays an important role, and it must be controlled to increase adsorption efficiency because pH influences the form of analytes in aqueous solution and the charge of the adsorbent surface. The effect of analyte pH was optimized from 2 to 10 by adjusting with 0.1 M NaOH and HCl solution. Fig. 5
c shows that pH 4 exhibited the maximum adsorption of the analytes. The dye (AR) attains negative charge following dissolution in water, while in lower pH (acidic medium) expected that positively charged sorbent tends to higher sorption of AR. A rise in pH results in a negative charge on the surface, which hinders the sorption of AR anionic dye through a repulsive force. For subsequent DSPE experiments, pH 4 was selected based on this consideration [1,20].

It is ideal that the elution solvent is strong enough to elute all the analytes from the sorbent. To select the best eluent for eluting the Allura red from the composite various organic and inorganic solvents and their mixtures were used including acetone, chloroform, DMF, propanol, ethanol, methanol, HCl, H2SO4, and HNO3 were examined as shown in Fig. 5
e . The best elution of Allura red is attained by using 1.0 M H2SO4 in ethanol which was selected as the elution solvent in the subsequent experiments due to its strongest dissolving ability and more ability for binding Allura red via hydrogen bonding. Due to increased interaction with the polar groups on the Alluar red, polar solvents exhibit better elution capability than nonpolar solvents. Moreover, eluent volumes ranging from 200 to 1000 µL were also studied. A minimum volume of 500 µL of eluent was found to be required for quantitative desorption. As a result, the subsequent experiments were performed with this eluent volume as the optimum [34–36].

It was also investigated how desorption time affects AR recovery. Experimental results revealed that desorption was completed within 5 min. Since CD@MOF is easily and rapidly collected from the solution using centrifuges, the whole extraction procedure can be completed in less than 30 min, and the analysis time is greatly reduced compared to conventional SPME.

In order to make the treatment process more cost-effective, exhaust sorbent can be reused and Allura red can be recovered. To regenerate the spent sorbent, an ethanol and water washing was employed owing to its simplicity and inexpensiveness after that, it was dried in an oven at 80 degrees Celsius. Fig. 5
f shows the respectable results of five Allura red sorption–desorption cycles conducted to determine the reusability of the CD@MOF. The sample was re-used for further process. After five cycles, AR's %ER dropped from 96 to 74, which suggests that it can be reused and regenerated [37].

Increasing concentrations of AR were contacted with CD@MOF nanocomposite in optimum adsorption conditions and then AR was desorpted in optimum conditions. A linear calibration graph was constructed in the AR concentration range from 0.30 to 7.00 nM with an equation of A = 0.1345C + 0.0185 and correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.9981) (Fig. 6 ). Where C is AR concentration in nM, and A is absorbance. Using slope of the calibration graph and blank solutions standard deviation. The limit of detection (LOD) of the proposed approach is 0.60 nM.

A statistical analysis was conducted to determine the accuracy and precision of the approach. To assessing relative standard deviation (RSD) as precision, five replicates were performed on three AR standard solutions (0.5, 3.0, and 7.0 nM). While a recovery (R%) calculation was carried out to assess the accuracy of the method, as shown in Table 1 .

Based on these results, the combination of CD@MOF and UA-DSPME using spectrophotometric method, it can achieve high sensitivity, linearity, and reproducibility for the analysis of real samples containing AR dye. It was estimated that the enrichment factor was 100.47 as the slope ratio after compared to before ultrasound assisted extraction.

A study of the effects of matrix ions on Allura red recovery on CD@MOF was also conducted. To determine the tolerance level of foreign ions, it was taken that the recovery percent would not exceed 100 ± 5 %. A high concentration of matrix ions in the solution did not affect Allura red recovery on CD@MOF as demonstrated in Table 2 . Under the experimental conditions, the ions normally present in water are not interfering. Moreover, some transition metals did not affect the recovery of Allura red on CD@MOF. According to these results, the proposed preconcentration method could be applied to samples containing some transition metals or highly saline samples.

Food samples were successfully tested using the developed method for determining AR dyes. AR contents of candy, jelly, strawberry flavored syrup, pomegranate flavored drink, energy drinks, drink water, commercial food colorant solution, and carbonated beverages were determined. A reliability and applicability test of the method was also conducted on real samples using the analyte addition method. The AR concentrations in real samples are presented in Table 3 as µg/g and µg/mL according to the samples physical (solid or liquid) form.

The analytical performance of the proposed UA-DSP-ME-UV–Visible method is shown in Table 4 . It was compared with other published solid phase extraction approaches. Based on the results, the present method has a narrow linear range and a lower DL. As a result of the dispersion of sorbent in bulk solutions, less time is needed to prepare samples, and extraction can be performed rapidly. In general, the present method developed by combining CD@MOF based UA-DSP-ME with UV–Visible is highly applicable and sufficient to provide a reproducible and sensitive approach for pre-concentration and estimation of AR dye in a variety of matrices that is highly sensitive and reproducible.

In conclusion, combination of the ultrasound-assisted DSP-ME and Ultraviolet–Visible spectrophotometry leads to presentation of sensitive extraction method based on CD@MOF as DSPME with high extraction recovery. As a result of the present method, the UA-DSPME extraction procedure is clearly shortened and simplified. This method also had a lower LOD value than previous studies (0.60 nM) with recovery values ranging from 98.44 to 102.41 %. Allura red content of analyzed solid food samples was 2.96, and 5.87 mM in candy and jelly, while it was found that analyzed beverages contained 8.43–42.77 nM AR. The approach may be applied in normal quality control laboratories applications without skilled analysts and complicated instruments. In addition, the interference study confirmed the applicability of the present system for monitoring AR at high concentration levels. The developed approach consumed less organic solvent and provided high extraction efficiency at a lower cost, and reduced extraction time, without compromising its environmental friendliness. The most important advantage of the proposed method is using of micro liters of solvent, in addition to the reusable ability of the synthesized CD@MOF that makes the treatment process more cost-effective, in which after five cycles extraction recovery dropped from 96 to 74 %.
No funding was received for this work.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrasonically synthesis of Mn- and Cu- @ ZnS-NPs-AC based ultrasound assisted extraction procedure and validation of a spectrophotometric method for a rapid preconcentration of Allura Red AC (E129) in food and water samples
Ultrason. Sonochem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Ostovan, H. Asadollahzadeh, M. Ghaedi, Ultrasonically synthesis of Mn- and Cu- @ ZnS-NPs-AC based ultrasound assisted extraction procedure and validation of a spectrophotometric method for a rapid preconcentration of Allura Red AC (E129) in food and water samples, Ultrasonics Sonochemistry, 43 (2018) 52-60 DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ultsonch.2018.01.002.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous preconcentration and determination of brilliant blue and sunset yellow in foodstuffs by solid-phase extraction combined UV-vis spectrophotometry
J. AOAC Int.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.T. Bişgin, Simultaneous preconcentration and determination of brilliant blue and sunset yellow in foodstuffs by solid-phase extraction combined UV-vis spectrophotometry, Journal of AOAC International, 101 (2018) 1850-1856 DOI: https://doi.org/10.5740/jaoacint.18-0089.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous spectrophotometric determination of brilliant blue and tartrazine in diverse sample matrices after solid phase extraction
J. AOAC Int.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.T. Bişgin, Simultaneous spectrophotometric determination of brilliant blue and tartrazine in diverse sample matrices after solid phase extraction, Journal of AOAC International, 103 (2020) 1478-1485 DOI: https://doi.org/10.1093/jaoacint/qsaa056.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Remediation of water pollution caused by pharmaceutical residues based on electrochemical separation and degradation technologies: a review
Environ. Int.

Chemosphere

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cloud point extraction and spectrophotometric determination of Allura red (E129) in foodstuffs
J. of the Inst. ofSci. and Technol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.T. BİŞGİN, Cloud point extraction and spectrophotometric determination of Allura red (E129) in foodstuffs, Journal of the Institute of Science and Technology, 8 (2018) 239-246 DOI: https://doi.org/10.21597/jist.397479.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spectrophotometric determination of trace levels of allura red in water samples after separation and preconcentration
Food Chem. Toxicol.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous spectrophotometric determination of synthetic dyes in food samples after cloud point extraction using multiple response optimizations
Talanta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Heidarizadi, R. Tabaraki, Simultaneous spectrophotometric determination of synthetic dyes in food samples after cloud point extraction using multiple response optimizations, Talanta, 148 (2016) 237-246 DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2015.10.075.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ion-pair solvent-based liquid–liquid microextraction and spectrophotometric determination of E102, E110, E124, E129 and E133 in confectioneries and beverages
J. Iran. Chem. Soc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.T. Bişgin, Ion-pair solvent-based liquid–liquid microextraction and spectrophotometric determination of E102, E110, E124, E129 and E133 in confectioneries and beverages, Journal of the Iranian Chemical Society, (2023) 1-12 DOI: https://doi.org/10.1007/s13738-023-02830-w.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Vortex-assisted sequential liquid-phase micro-extraction of E127 and E129 in foodstuffs and pharmaceuticals
